publications.editedComment: The panel wakes only for administrators and initializes the Pack store before selecting its first manifest. A pack may be built in the editor or imported as JSON. Each item moves through local states for record creation, art generation, completion, or error, with store methods owning every operation. `Create all remaining records` is deliberately sequential because the generation backend is shared. A complete pack may be marked ready, but the page states that this changes only local build bookkeeping; publication and storefront work remain outside the panel. Good machinery knows the difference between finishing a crate and opening the market.

This front page points toward a train already running elsewhere. On mount it initializes the Scenario store, derives the total count, and shows at most the first three scenario titles. Every listed scenario and the primary button route to `/stories`; no story is created, narrated, or edited in this Component. `ProjectFrontPage` supplies the broader project shell from the fallback configuration, whose deliverables distinguish the reused Stories engine from a still-future dedicated Storymaker flow and multi-author sessions. The ghost announces the platform and the waiting carriages, but the journey begins only after entering the existing Stories studio.

Platform status: the Scenario store is initialized on mount, `totalScenarios` reports how many are available, and `recentScenarios` takes the first three records for display. Their links and the main action all depart for `/stories`. The project shell describes Storymaker's intended cast, setting, reward, and narrator direction, while its deliverables clearly label the existing Stories/scenario engine as done and a dedicated flow as next. No route is dispatched automatically and no scenario mutation occurs here. This page is signage and a timetable for the current studio, not a second storytelling engine.

A very short score, played by two instruments. `ProjectFrontPage` receives the `media-watchlist` slug and fallback project configuration; its interactive slot contains `WatchlistBrowse`, which owns the actual browse surface. The local configuration supplies title, channel, icon, descriptive sections, reported project statistics, completed deliverables, and the next planned detail/editor and CSV work. There is no store access, API request, filtering logic, or media mutation in this wrapper. The orchestration is sound because it does not steal the solo: project framing remains here, watchlist behavior remains in the child.

There are only two living pieces in this little house: the project-front wrapper and `WatchlistBrowse`. The fallback config describes queueing, tracking, imported viewing history, the data model, browse and stats work, plus future detail editing and CSV export. The interactive child is where current media behavior must be inspected; this file does not fetch, sort, save, or review an entry. It gives the project a name, route identity, sections, statistics, and deliverable labels. We searched every cupboard. No hidden watchlist state is buried in the walls.

The deck advances under a strict gesture order. `swipedCount` selects the active item and the next two visual depth cards. Pointer capture measures horizontal displacement; crossing 80 pixels commands a left skip or right selection, while a shorter drag returns to center. Right movement emits the current id before the card flies away; left movement does not. The Info control emits the id and then advances without a second selection. A 320ms timeout completes each departure, and reset restores all local gesture state. The Component does not persist likes or open detail routes itself. It issues one `select` signal and maintains one temporary parade of cards.

The deck makes one item visible, keeps two upcoming cards as a depth preview, and translates horizontal pointer motion into a temporary rotation. Crossing the threshold sends the active card left or right; a right swipe emits its id, while a left swipe only advances. Buttons call the same paths, and the center Info action emits before advancing right. Progress is local: dots are used for twelve or fewer items, otherwise a numeric position appears. When the stack ends, reset returns to the first item. This Component helps a visitor make sequential choices, but the parent must decide what a selected id means and how to preserve it.

reviewer.name: Lord Vance of the Abyssal Court

publications.editedComment: Three gates lead to the same coronation. Gallery mode loads collections and ArtImages, filters mature records according to the User store, resolves a single preview, and applies the chosen image. Upload delegates to `image-upload`; Generate delegates to `generate-button` and receives its completed ArtImage. The Upload store is configured for the current user's avatar target, while successful application refreshes the preview, reports status, and emits `selected`. A virtual All images collection joins the real collection options, and broken thumbnails fall back through the source helpers. The court chooses an existing ArtImage identity; it does not store arbitrary image URLs as a separate avatar system.

The question 'which image represents me?' is answered through one ArtImage record, despite three acquisition tabs. Gallery combines an All images view with real collections, hydrates records, excludes mature images unless the User preference permits them, and offers one selected preview. Upload is the shared upload Component; Generate is the shared generation Component. Their results converge on the same avatar-application path, which updates status, preview, and the `selected` event. `dismissible` controls only the close affordance. The existential choice is durable because the User and Upload stores own the target—not because this picker invents another profile state.
